blocks.content: The barn doors had been chained once. The chain was still there, but the padlock was gone — cut off clean. The doors swung open on hinges that screamed loud enough to hear across the yard.
Inside: cool, dim, smelling of old hay and machine oil. Light came through gaps in the boards in long dust-thick beams. And there, on the far wall, hanging from two iron hooks:
Eli made a sound that was less a word than a verdict on history.

blocks.speaker: Eli
blocks.content: I told you. I told every single one of you. By name. I said saddle.
He walked toward it like it was something holy, put his hand on the cracked leather, and looked back at them.

blocks.speaker: Eli
blocks.content: I want it noted. In the same record where we noted the rusted rungs.

blocks.speaker: Daisy
blocks.content: Noted. You were right about the saddle.

Eli patted it once and stepped back. Daisy had drifted to the shelf along the side wall — old paint cans, a pair of work gloves, a coffee mug with its handle broken off. She picked it up, turned it over, set it back down.
